case_studies.title: Referral Pathway for Progressive Spinal Deformity in a School-Aged Child
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural district in India was identified during a school health screening under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) program. She complained of mild back pain and her teacher noted that her shoulders appeared uneven. Upon further questioning, her mother reported noticing a visible curve in her back over the past year, which seemed to be worsening. There was no history of trauma, neurological symptoms, or systemic illness. On examination at the district hospital, the child had a right thoracic prominence on forward bending (Adam’s test positive) and a visible rib hump. Skin examination revealed no midline dimples or hairy patches. Height was in the 75th percentile, and her weight was appropriate for age. A plain X-ray of the spine showed a right-sided thoracic curvature with a Cobb angle of 28 degrees. No vertebral anomalies were noted. The clinician documented the findings and explained the need for referral to a tertiary center for further evaluation and possible bracing. The family was counseled about the importance of early intervention and provided with a written referral, including X-ray images and clinical notes.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the structured referral pathway for scoliosis, including documentation, public health program roles, and criteria for escalation to tertiary care.

case_studies.title: Referral for Progressive Spinal Deformity in a School-Aged Child
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural district in India was identified with a visible spinal curvature during a school health screening conducted under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK). Her parents report noticing the asymmetry six months ago, which has progressively worsened. She denies back pain but mentions occasional difficulty carrying her school bag. On examination at the district hospital, she has a right thoracic prominence on forward bending (Adam’s test) and uneven shoulder heights. Skin over the spine is normal, with no hairy patches or dimples. Neurological examination reveals normal strength and reflexes in all extremities. A locally obtained standing X-ray shows a Cobb angle of 35 degrees in the thoracic spine. The family was advised referral to a tertiary center, but they express concerns about travel expenses and missing school. They were provided with a detailed referral note and contact information for teleconsultation to discuss further steps with a pediatric orthopedic specialist.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the need for early referral of progressive spinal deformities detected in community settings, emphasizing structured documentation and public health program pathways to ensure timely specialist care.

case_studies.title: Referral of a Child with Progressive Spinal Deformity
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural village in Uttar Pradesh was identified during a school health screening under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) program. The visiting health team noted that her right shoulder appeared higher than the left and her back had an abnormal curve. Her parents reported noticing the asymmetry over the past 6 months, which seemed to worsen as she grew taller. She occasionally complains of mild back pain but denies any weakness, numbness, or difficulty walking. On examination at the district hospital, she had a visible right thoracic prominence on forward bending. No skin markers, such as dimples or hairy patches, were noted. Neurological examination was normal. An X-ray of the spine was done locally, showing a thoracic curve measuring 35 degrees. The family was advised to seek further evaluation at a tertiary pediatric orthopedic center, but they expressed concerns about travel costs and time away from work. The local physician prepared a detailed referral note and coordinated a teleconsultation with a specialist to discuss the urgency and next steps.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of structured referral pathways and public health programs in the early detection and escalation of progressive spinal deformities in resource-limited settings.

case_studies.title: Referral of a Progressive Spinal Deformity in a School-Aged Child
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural district in India was identified during a routine school health screening under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) program. The health worker noted a visible asymmetry in her shoulders and a prominent rib hump when she bent forward. The child reported no pain but mentioned that her school uniform had started to fit unevenly over the past year. Her parents, farmers with limited access to healthcare, were unaware of the condition. On examination at the district hospital, the pediatrician confirmed a right thoracic curve with a mild lumbar compensatory curve. The Adam's forward bend test was positive, and a scoliometer reading showed an angle of 12 degrees. No skin markers, neurological deficits, or signs of systemic illness were noted. A plain X-ray of the spine revealed a thoracic Cobb angle of 25 degrees. The pediatrician provided a referral note for a tertiary center with pediatric orthopedic expertise, emphasizing the need for early evaluation due to the girl's significant growth potential and progressive curve. The family was counseled on the referral process, and back photographs were taken for documentation.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of structured referral pathways and public health programs in identifying and escalating care for progressive spinal deformities in children.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SCREEN-DETECTED ADOLESCENT IDIOPATHIC SCOLIOSIS WITH GROWTH REMAINING
subtopics.category: CLINICAL CASE APPLICATIONS IN PEDIATRIC SPINAL DEFORMITY
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Screen-detected adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) with growth remaining must be assessed in the context of skeletal maturity and curve severity. A 13-year-old girl with a 25-degree right thoracic curve at Risser 0 and a 7-degree trunk rotation exemplifies a lower moderate curve with substantial growth remaining, necessitating structured surveillance rather than discharge. Progression risk is highest during the pubertal growth spurt, making early identification and monitoring critical.
- **Clinical application:** Initial management involves repeat clinical examination and radiography after a short interval to monitor curve progression. Counseling families about the dynamic nature of treatment, including potential transitions to bracing or surgery, reduces surprise and improves adherence to follow-up. For patients progressing despite bracing, clinicians must verify brace fit, in-brace correction, and adherence before labeling it as treatment failure. Persistent progression despite a well-fitted brace signals the need for escalation, such as surgical consultation.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Short-interval follow-up is essential to detect progression during rapid growth phases, especially in patients at Risser 0 or 1. Transparent communication about the likelihood of treatment escalation, including bracing or surgery, prepares families for potential next steps. Adherence to follow-up schedules and early intervention can prevent significant curve progression and associated complications.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PAINFUL OR RAPIDLY PROGRESSIVE CURVES REQUIRING URGENT INVESTIGATION
subtopics.category: CLINICAL CASE APPLICATIONS IN PEDIATRIC SPINAL DEFORMITY
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Painful or rapidly progressive scoliosis requires urgent investigation to rule out structural secondary pathology. Red flags include nocturnal thoracic pain, rapid curve progression, atypical features like left thoracic curves, marked stiffness, or associated neurological symptoms such as bowel, bladder, sensory, or gait changes. These presentations should not be managed as routine idiopathic scoliosis, even if the Cobb angle is moderate.
- **Clinical application:** Urgent advanced imaging, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), is essential when red flags are present to evaluate for conditions like osteoid osteoma, infection, or neoplasm. For example, an adolescent boy with new nocturnal thoracic pain, focal tenderness, and a recently noticed curve requires immediate imaging rather than observation. Recognizing atypical patterns and escalating workup early prevents diagnostic delays and avoids mismanagement based on assumptions of benign idiopathic scoliosis.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Prolonged reassurance based solely on adolescence or mild Cobb angle can lead to missed diagnoses in cases with atypical features. Early targeted investigations protect against months of delay and ensure timely identification of underlying pathology. Clinicians must prioritize imaging in cases of painful scoliosis or neurologic involvement, as these are imaging problems first and observation problems second.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MISCLASSIFICATION MEASUREMENT ERROR AND MISSED RED-FLAG PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.category: PREVENTION OF DIAGNOSTIC AND FOLLOW-UP ERRORS IN SPINAL DEFORMITY CARE
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Misclassification and measurement errors are critical contributors to suboptimal outcomes in pediatric spinal deformity care. Atypical or painful curves misclassified as idiopathic can delay MRI or specialist referral, while overreacting to normal Cobb measurement variability may lead to unnecessary escalation. Relying solely on Risser stage for brace discontinuation risks premature cessation before growth is complete. Technical errors, such as nonstandard radiographs or inconsistent end vertebra selection, further compromise serial comparisons.
- **Clinical application:** Clinicians should revisit four key questions at every visit: is the diagnosis still correct, is the imaging comparable, is the growth estimate current, and are treatment goals being met in real life? This structured approach helps prevent errors like prolonged observation in nonambulatory children losing sitting balance or prescribing braces for severe neuromuscular curves unlikely to respond. Written measurement protocols and consistent team-based assessments improve reliability over time. Functional reviews should complement technical accuracy during all encounters.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Adolescents with large residual curves should not be discharged without clear long-term follow-up advice to monitor progression. Psychosocial barriers, such as unrealistic brace wear expectations, must be addressed proactively to ensure adherence. Imaging must be standardized, with pelvic obliquity corrected and end vertebra selection consistent, to allow reliable serial comparisons. Linking treatment decisions to the child’s functional status, rather than radiographic findings alone, ensures holistic care and minimizes long-term complications.

case_studies.title: Misclassification and Missed Red Flags in a Child with Spinal Deformity
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural Indian village presents to a primary care clinic with a 6-month history of progressive back asymmetry noticed by her parents. The family initially attributed it to poor posture, but she recently started complaining of intermittent back pain, especially at night, which prompted the visit. There is no history of trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her school performance and daily activities are mildly affected due to discomfort. A local practitioner previously diagnosed idiopathic scoliosis based on a single anteroposterior spinal X-ray, reporting a Cobb angle of 25 degrees. On examination, she has a visible right thoracic prominence on forward bending, mild tenderness over the mid-thoracic spine, and normal lower extremity strength. No café-au-lait spots or other neurocutaneous markers are noted. The X-ray provided for review lacks proper standardization, with unclear end vertebra selection. The family seeks advice on whether bracing is necessary, but no recent imaging or growth assessment has been performed. The clinic lacks advanced imaging facilities, and referral pathways are limited.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the impact of misclassification and measurement errors in scoliosis care and identify red-flag symptoms, such as nighttime pain, that warrant timely specialist referral and advanced imaging.

subtopics.subtopic_title: AGE- AND TREATMENT-SPECIFIC FOLLOW-UP INTERVALS AND RADIOGRAPHIC SURVEILLANCE
subtopics.category: LONG-TERM FOLLOW-UP AND TRANSITION TO ADULT CARE
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Follow-up intervals for scoliosis must be individualized based on age, treatment modality, and growth velocity, rather than adhering to fixed schedules. Early-onset scoliosis requires reviews every 2–3 months due to rapid changes in curve behavior and cast fit. Juvenile idiopathic scoliosis typically necessitates reassessment every 4–6 months during growth, while adolescents with small, stable curves can be reviewed clinically every 6 months unless examination findings indicate the need for radiographs.
- **Clinical application:** Moderate curves and brace-treated adolescents require imaging every 4–6 months during peak growth, with intervals extended as skeletal maturity approaches. Postoperative follow-up involves early radiographic assessment followed by annual surveillance once alignment and healing stabilize. Residual larger curves after brace weaning or surgery demand prolonged observation, even as maturity is reached. Families must be educated to seek earlier review for symptoms such as new pain, neurological changes, respiratory difficulty, rapid growth, or brace intolerance.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Serial Cobb angle measurements should be interpreted cautiously, as changes below 5 degrees may reflect measurement variability rather than true curve progression. Effective surveillance hinges on consistent radiographic techniques, up-to-date assessments of skeletal maturity, and clear communication of return precautions. As growth slows, follow-up intervals can be gradually extended, but careful monitoring remains essential for patients with significant residual curves.

case_studies.title: Scoliosis Follow-Up in a Brace-Treated Adolescent
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a rural community in India presents for a scheduled follow-up at a tertiary care hospital. She was diagnosed with juvenile idiopathic scoliosis a year ago and has been wearing a thoracolumbar brace for the past 8 months. Her initial Cobb angle was 28 degrees, and her last radiograph 4 months ago showed a stable curve at 30 degrees. She reports no pain or neurological symptoms but mentions a recent growth spurt over the past 3 months. On examination, her height has increased by 4 cm since the last visit. The brace appears tight, and there are mild areas of skin irritation over her iliac crests. A forward bend test reveals a rib hump similar to previous findings. There are no signs of respiratory distress or weakness. The family asks whether another radiograph is necessary at this visit, given her growth spurt and the tightness of the brace.
case_studies.learning_objective: Determine appropriate follow-up intervals and imaging needs for a brace-treated adolescent with scoliosis, considering growth velocity and clinical findings.

case_studies.title: Scoliosis Follow-Up in a Rapidly Growing Adolescent
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a rural area presents to the outpatient clinic for follow-up of idiopathic scoliosis. She was diagnosed 8 months ago with a thoracic curve measuring 28 degrees on Cobb angle and has been using a brace prescribed at a tertiary center. Her parents report that she has grown rapidly in the past 6 months, and her school uniform no longer fits. She complains of occasional discomfort in her back but denies respiratory issues or neurological symptoms. On examination, her height has increased by 6 cm since her last visit, and her weight is appropriate for age. The Adam’s forward bend test reveals asymmetry in her thoracic spine, which appears more pronounced than during her previous visit. The brace shows signs of wear, and her mother mentions difficulty in ensuring consistent use due to discomfort. No fever or acute complications are noted.
case_studies.learning_objective: Determine appropriate follow-up intervals and imaging needs for scoliosis surveillance during peak growth periods, considering brace tolerance and signs of curve progression.

case_studies.title: Scoliosis Follow-Up in a Rapidly Growing Adolescent
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a rural area in India presents for a follow-up visit at the orthopaedic clinic. She was diagnosed with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis six months ago, with a Cobb angle of 28 degrees. She was started on a thoracolumbar brace, which she wears for 20 hours daily. Her mother reports that the girl has had a recent growth spurt and has outgrown her school uniform in the past three months. The patient denies back pain, difficulty breathing, or neurological symptoms. On examination, her height has increased by 6 cm since her last visit. There is mild asymmetry of the shoulders and waist, but her brace appears snug and well-fitted. Neurological examination is normal. The family inquires about the need for imaging and the timing of her next follow-up, as they had to travel 150 km to attend this appointment.
case_studies.learning_objective: Determine appropriate follow-up intervals and the need for radiographic surveillance in a brace-treated adolescent with scoliosis during a period of rapid growth.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SENSOR-EQUIPPED BRACES VERTEBRAL BODY TETHERING AND MAGNETIC ROD SYSTEMS
subtopics.category: EMERGING GROWTH-MODULATION AND DEVICE-BASED TREATMENT OPTIONS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Sensor-equipped braces, vertebral body tethering, and magnetic rod systems represent emerging growth-modulation technologies for paediatric scoliosis management. These innovations do not replace the fundamentals of accurate classification, growth assessment, and timely referral, but rather complement them. Wear sensors can differentiate poor adherence from true brace failure, aiding in counseling for challenging cases, though routine access to such systems remains limited.
- **Clinical application:** Vertebral body tethering is a motion-preserving option for skeletally immature adolescents with flexible progressive idiopathic curves who fail or cannot tolerate bracing. Success requires adequate remaining growth and curve flexibility, but risks include tether breakage, overcorrection, pulmonary complications, and potential conversion to fusion. Magnetically controlled growing rods reduce the need for repeated open lengthening procedures in severe early-onset scoliosis but demand intensive follow-up due to risks such as rod breakage, distraction failure, and revision needs.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Long-term surveillance, realistic counseling, and careful patient selection are critical for all device-based treatments. Families must understand the risks and limitations, including the investigational nature of adaptive braces and guided-growth systems. These technologies are best utilized in experienced pediatric spine centers with established revision pathways, imaging protocols, and multidisciplinary support. The novelty of devices should not overshadow the importance of matching the child’s curve pattern, maturity profile, and follow-up capacity.

case_studies.title: 12-Year-Old Girl with Progressive Spinal Deformity
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a rural area in India presents to a tertiary care hospital with concerns about worsening spinal curvature noticed by her parents over the past year. She reports occasional back pain but denies any neurological deficits or systemic symptoms. Her growth has been normal, and she recently had her first menstrual period. Physical examination reveals a visible right thoracic prominence on forward bending (Adam’s test) and a mild left lumbar curve. A scoliometer reading shows a 15-degree tilt at the thoracic level. Her height and weight are at the 25th percentile. A recent chest X-ray obtained locally shows a 35-degree right thoracic Cobb angle. The family reports difficulty in ensuring consistent brace wear due to discomfort and lack of awareness. They inquire about alternative treatments after hearing about newer technologies from other patients. The nearest specialized spine center is 200 km away, raising concerns about follow-up feasibility.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Progressive Spinal Deformity and Poor Brace Compliance
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a semi-urban area presents to the outpatient clinic with a noticeable curvature of her spine, first observed by her parents 8 months ago. Despite being prescribed a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) brace 6 months ago, her parents report inconsistent usage due to discomfort and social stigma at school. Over the past 2 months, her posture has worsened, with asymmetry of her shoulders and waist becoming more visible. She denies pain, weakness, or difficulty breathing. On examination, she has a right thoracic prominence on forward bending, a shoulder height difference of 2 cm, and a pelvic tilt. Her height and weight are at the 50th percentile for her age, but her Risser score is 1, indicating significant remaining growth potential. A recent standing spinal X-ray shows a right thoracic curve measuring 42 degrees. The family expresses concern about the progression of her condition and seeks advice on alternative treatments.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Progressive Spinal Deformity and Brace Adherence Concerns
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a semi-urban area presents to the outpatient clinic with concerns about worsening spinal asymmetry over the past 8 months. She reports no pain but mentions difficulty fitting into her school uniform and occasional teasing from peers about her posture. Her parents state she was diagnosed with scoliosis a year ago and prescribed a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO). They admit she struggles to wear the brace consistently, especially during school hours, citing discomfort and embarrassment. On examination, she has an obvious right thoracic rib hump on forward bending and mild shoulder asymmetry. Her height and weight are at the 25th percentile for age. A recent standing spinal X-ray (AP and lateral views) reveals a 38-degree right thoracic curve with visible progression compared to a 30-degree curve noted 6 months ago. The family expresses concern about the worsening deformity but is hesitant about surgical intervention. They inquire about alternative options that might preserve her mobility and avoid fusion procedures.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate the role of sensor-equipped braces, vertebral body tethering, and magnetic rod systems in managing progressive scoliosis in skeletally immature adolescents, emphasizing patient selection, adherence challenges, and safety considerations.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Progressive Spinal Deformity and Poor Brace Compliance
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India presents with worsening asymmetry of her back over the past 8 months. Her parents report noticing a visible curve in her spine and uneven shoulders, which have progressively worsened despite her prescribed use of a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) brace for the past 6 months. On further questioning, the family admits to inconsistent brace use as the patient finds it uncomfortable, especially during school hours. She denies pain, weakness, or difficulty walking but feels self-conscious about her appearance. Examination reveals a right thoracic curve with a rib hump on forward bending and a mild lumbar compensatory curve. The curve appears flexible, and her neurological exam is normal. A recent X-ray shows a Cobb angle of 45 degrees, increased from 38 degrees three months ago. The family expresses concern about the need for surgery and asks about alternative options.
case_studies.learning_objective: To evaluate the role of sensor-equipped braces, vertebral body tethering, and magnetic rod systems in managing progressive scoliosis in adolescents, emphasizing patient selection, adherence challenges, and referral timing.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Progressive Scoliosis and Brace Compliance Concerns
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old girl from a semi-urban area presents to the outpatient clinic with her mother, concerned about worsening asymmetry in her shoulders and waist over the past 6 months. She was diagnosed with idiopathic scoliosis a year ago and prescribed a thoracolumbar brace, which she has reportedly been wearing for 8–10 hours daily. However, her mother admits that the child finds the brace uncomfortable and often removes it at school. On examination, the patient has a prominent right thoracic rib hump on forward bending and a noticeable shoulder height discrepancy. Her height has increased significantly in the past year, and her Tanner stage is 2. A recent standing spinal X-ray shows a 35-degree thoracic Cobb angle, up from 25 degrees six months ago. The family expresses concerns about the progression and asks about alternative treatments, as the patient is reluctant to continue bracing. They are seeking guidance on the next steps and whether surgical options are necessary.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the role of sensor-equipped braces, vertebral body tethering, and magnetic rod systems in managing progressive scoliosis, and assess when to consider advanced interventions based on growth potential, curve flexibility, and brace adherence.

subtopics.subtopic_title: BRACE WEANING ADULT PROGRESSION RISK AND TRANSFER OF CARE
subtopics.category: LONG-TERM FOLLOW-UP AND TRANSITION TO ADULT CARE
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Follow-up for scoliosis should extend until skeletal maturity and, for selected children, beyond it. Curves below 30 degrees at maturity are typically stable, while those above 50 degrees may progress slowly in adulthood. Brace weaning must be gradual, guided by stable radiographs and reliable maturity markers, rather than relying solely on a single visit or Risser stage. A confirmatory radiograph post-brace discontinuation is essential to detect early rebound.
- **Clinical application:** Adolescents with moderate residual deformity, congenital scoliosis, neuromuscular disease, prior growth-friendly instrumentation, or persistent pain should not be abruptly discharged at age 18. A structured transfer summary should include curve history, operative details, maturity status, neurologic findings, and future risk considerations. Transition planning offers an opportunity to emphasize weight management, exercise, and the need for medical review if pain, imbalance, or neurologic symptoms arise. Long-term care is selective but critical for patients with residual deformity or non-idiopathic conditions.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Children transitioning to adulthood with residual imbalance, prior long fusion, or chronic neuromuscular disease require adult-oriented follow-up to monitor for late-onset pain or mechanical complications. Transition should be framed as continuity of surveillance rather than the conclusion of spine care. Reinforcing the importance of seeking timely evaluation for new symptoms ensures proactive management of potential late complications. Structured transition ensures smoother care continuity and mitigates risks of neglecting long-term issues.

case_studies.title: Brace Weaning and Transition of Care in Adolescent Scoliosis
case_studies.vignette: A 17-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India presents for a follow-up visit at the orthopaedic clinic. She has been treated for idiopathic scoliosis since the age of 12, with a thoracic curve that measured 42 degrees at its peak. She has been wearing a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) for 18 hours daily, which was gradually reduced to 8 hours over the past year. On her last visit six months ago, her curve measured 35 degrees, and her Risser stage was 4. She is now requesting to stop using the brace entirely, as she finds it cumbersome during her final year of school. She denies any back pain, but her mother mentions occasional complaints of fatigue after prolonged sitting. Examination reveals no significant asymmetry in shoulder or pelvic alignment, and her forward bend test shows a mild rib hump. A recent radiograph shows a stable curve of 35 degrees with no significant progression since the last visit. The family inquires about the need for further follow-up after she turns 18, as they live far from the clinic.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of gradual brace weaning, confirmatory radiographs, and structured transition of care for adolescents with residual scoliosis to mitigate risks of adult progression or late complications.

case_studies.title: Brace Weaning and Transition to Adult Care in Scoliosis
case_studies.vignette: A 16-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India presents for a follow-up visit at the paediatric orthopaedic clinic. She was diagnosed with idiopathic scoliosis at age 12 and has been wearing a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) for the past four years. Her initial Cobb angle was 38 degrees, which improved to 28 degrees after consistent brace use. She has recently achieved menarche, and her Risser stage is 4 on recent imaging. Her family is eager to discontinue the brace as she is preparing for her final school exams and finds it cumbersome. She denies pain, imbalance, or neurologic symptoms. On examination, her shoulders are level, and the Adam’s forward bend test shows mild thoracic prominence. A recent radiograph taken in-brace shows the curve remains stable at 28 degrees. The family inquires about the need for further follow-up and whether she can stop wearing the brace entirely. They express concern about whether her condition might worsen in adulthood.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of gradual brace weaning, structured transition to adult care, and long-term surveillance for scoliosis patients with residual deformity.

case_studies.title: Brace Weaning and Transition of Care in Adolescent Scoliosis
case_studies.vignette: A 16-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India, previously diagnosed with idiopathic scoliosis, presents for follow-up. She has been wearing a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) for the past three years, with her curve initially measuring 38 degrees. Over the past year, her brace wear has been gradually reduced under guidance. Her last radiograph, taken six months ago, showed a stable curve at 32 degrees. She has now reached Risser stage 5. She reports mild lower back pain after prolonged sitting during her board exam preparations but denies any new neurologic symptoms. On examination, her posture is balanced, and forward bend test reveals no significant changes in rib prominence. Neurologic examination is normal. The family inquires about stopping the brace and whether further follow-up is necessary, as they perceive her condition to be stable. They express concerns about the financial burden of ongoing care. No radiographs have been performed since the last visit.

case_studies.title: Brace Weaning and Transition Planning in Adolescent Scoliosis
case_studies.vignette: A 17-year-old girl from a rural area in India presents for a routine follow-up at the orthopaedic clinic. She was diagnosed with idiopathic scoliosis at age 12 and has been managed with a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) for the past 5 years. Her most recent radiograph, taken 6 months ago, showed a stable 38-degree thoracic curve. Menarche occurred at age 13, and her Risser stage is 5. She has been weaning off the brace over the last 3 months, wearing it only at night. She reports occasional mild back discomfort but denies any recent falls, weakness, or changes in balance. On examination, her shoulder levels are symmetric, and there is no visible rib hump or pelvic obliquity. Neurological examination is unremarkable. The family inquires if she can stop using the brace entirely and whether further follow-up is necessary, as she will soon be moving to a nearby city for college. They are concerned about the financial burden of continued care.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess the need for continued monitoring and structured transition of care in adolescents with residual spinal deformity post-brace weaning, emphasizing the importance of long-term surveillance and patient education to mitigate risks of adult progression
